verses.text: Reuben was Israel's firstborn, but he forfeited his birthright by sin, and leadership passed to Judah, the double portion to Joseph. Position among God's people is not held by birth alone. Privilege squandered by sin is given to another.

Reuben was Israel's firstborn, but he forfeited his birthright by sin, and leadership passed to Judah, the double portion to Joseph. Position among God's people is not held by birth alone. Privilege squandered by sin is given to another.

Reuben's descendants are traced down to Beerah, whom Assyria carried away captive. The tribe's record ends in exile. Even a firstborn tribe, unfaithful, is swept from its land; heritage cannot save where holiness is abandoned.

Reuben's descendants are traced down to Beerah, whom Assyria carried away captive. The tribe's record ends in exile. Even a firstborn tribe, unfaithful, is swept from its land; heritage cannot save where holiness is abandoned.

Reuben's descendants are traced down to Beerah, whom Assyria carried away captive. The tribe's record ends in exile. Even a firstborn tribe, unfaithful, is swept from its land; heritage cannot save where holiness is abandoned.

Reuben's chiefs and their wide eastern pastures are listed, won in war against the Hagarites. The tribe prospered in flocks and territory. Yet earthly increase was no guard against the judgment their later sin would invite.

Gad's families settle beside Reuben in Bashan and Gilead. The eastern tribes are carefully recorded. Though they dwelt far from Jerusalem and the temple, they were still counted among the people of God.

Gad's families settle beside Reuben in Bashan and Gilead. The eastern tribes are carefully recorded. Though they dwelt far from Jerusalem and the temple, they were still counted among the people of God.

Gad's genealogy is reckoned in the days of Jotham and Jeroboam. The Chronicler dates and preserves even the remote tribes' records. God keeps the account of the scattered and the distant among His own.

The eastern tribes field valiant warriors and rout the Hagarites, 'for they cried to God in the battle... because they put their trust in him.' Victory came not by numbers but by trusting prayer in the fight itself.

Half-Manasseh grows mighty, then turns to the gods of the land, so God stirs up Assyria to carry them into exile. Their strength could not save them from their sin. Apostasy, not weakness, brought down a numerous and famous people.
